46746989.title: 3.1 Intro to Collection Classes
46746989.transcript: Hello everyone. Welcome to module three of Java Collections Framework course. In this module, we will dive deep into the world of Java collection classes, which are fundamental part of our Java programming language. So let's start with an introduction to collection classes. What are they and why do we need them. So what are collection classes? Collection classes in Java provide a standardized way to handle groups of objects. Think of them as toolbox filled with different kinds of containers, each designed for a specific purpose like holding, organizing, and manipulating data. So basically what it means is in Java, a collection is an object that represents a group of objects known as its elements. The Java Collections Framework provides several interfaces and classes that allow you to work with these groups of objects in a structured and efficient manner. And why do we need to use these collections? So before the introduction of Java collections, developers had to write custom code to manage collections of objects. This was not only time consuming, but also error prone. Thus, the collection framework provides a unified architecture for representing and manipulating collections, making it easier to use and more powerful. So basically, using collections allows you to focus more on the logic of your application rather than worrying about how to manage data. They provide reusable data structures such as lists, sets, and maps that can be easily integrated into your applications. So before going to even more deep dive into our collection implementations, now let's take a quick look at our core interfaces of collection framework, which we have already explored in our previous module. So basically, the collection framework is built on several core interfaces that define the behavior of different types of collections. The most important ones are list, set and map. We will be having Q, but mostly we are using these three. So what exactly is the list? So here list is an ordered collection also known as a sequence list, can contain duplicate elements and provide positional access to elements, which means here we can contain similar values two times or any number of times. It won't give any error or any exceptions for that, and list also provides the index based access to its elements. And whereas a set, it is a collection that does not allow duplicate elements. Unlike list, set doesn't allow duplicate elements in its implementations or in its collection. Set is ideal for when you want to store unique values. So when we add similar item two times, consider it as a single entity and stores as only one element and just ignores the second element, which makes it a unique collection. 46747147.transcript: Hello everyone. Welcome back to our Java Collections Framework course. So caching can significantly improve performance by reducing the time taken to access data that frequently used. Let's see how can we implement this using map? So first of all, let's start with what exactly is caching by definition? Simply put, caching is the process of storing a computed result so that next time you need it, you don't have to compute it again. Instead, you retrieve it from the cache, which is faster. So overall you can say that caching equals to storing computed results for quick access. Now where do maps fit into this? A map in Java stores key value pairs, right? The key is often the request or input to a method, and value is the result you want to cache. Whenever you need a need result, you first cache the map to see if the result is already available. If it is available, we return the cached value. If not, we calculate the result and store it in map and return the fresh value. So here. Synchronizing means it's like putting a lock on the door so that only one thread can access our ArrayList at a time. For example, you can do something like this. So for our ArrayList we can synchronize it like this by calling collections dot synchronized list and passing whatever list implementation we want.
